title: The Divided Mile
tonic_progression: Gm -> Dm -> Am (Phrygian b2) -> Em -> Bm (Dorian raised-6) -> F#m (Phrygian-dominant flavor) -> C#m -> Abm / G#m -> Ebm -> Bbm -> Fm -> Gm
narrative_arc: Twelve tracks read as a single night spent inside the divided city circa 1983, one citizen of the East side moving from a watchful gray dawn through the long surveilled day to a watchful gray dawn again, the city split down its own spine by a concrete line they can see but never cross. Tracks 1-3 are the gray waking of the East: a single sodium-amber streetlamp still burns over a wet Plattenbau forecourt at first colorless light as a lone Polivoks-style drone opens on Gm and a faint distant-radio voice bleeds through tape hiss from a Western station the listener is not supposed to receive (1), the citizen rides a near-empty S-Bahn carriage over the elevated track past the back walls of buildings whose windows on the far side face West (2), and they walk the long gray avenue toward the wall-zone where the death-strip raked sand and the watchtower silhouette first enter the frame under a flat pewter sky (3). Tracks 4-6 are the wall itself and the checkpoint: they reach the inner wall and the raked control-strip with its tripwires and dog-runs and the searchlight arm sweeping the sand at intervals (4), they pass the documents-and-mirror-pole inspection at the sector crossing as a Polivoks lead reads the wait in clinical Phrygian over a stamp-and-teletype rhythm (5), and they stand at the actual line at midday where through a gap they can see the bright commercial neon and traffic of the Western sector twenty meters and a whole world away, the album's first long gaze across the divide (6). Tracks 7-9 are the surveillance crisis: a tail picks them up on the homeward avenue and a gray car keeps three doors back as the watch tightens to the album's BPM ceiling (7), they sit in a bare interview room under a single hanging bulb answering questions over twenty-minute pauses while a reel-to-reel turns behind a one-way pane (8), and the questioning lifts as abruptly as it began and they are returned to the street with a folded paper and no explanation, the held breath released (9). Tracks 10-12 are the long walk home and the second dawn: they ride the S-Bahn back over the elevated track as a cold sleet streaks the glass and the Western lights blur across the strip one last time (10), they climb the unlit Plattenbau stairwell to a single room where the contraband Western radio is switched off and its dial-glow dies into tape hiss (11), and at the second gray dawn the same sodium lamp burns over the same wet forecourt as the opening drone returns on Polivoks lead one octave down and unresolved, the divided mile walked once through and waiting to be walked again. The night rings closed on the same tonic that opened it (G minor), but the citizen arrives at the second dawn having seen across the line and carried nothing back across it, and the forecourt is the same forecourt and the wall is still standing.

recurring_motifs.motif: Crossing-signal cell - a four-note Polivoks-style sawtooth lead figure that climbs a perfect fourth and falls a minor second (1 -> 4 -> b3 -> 1, the reach across and the small failure to land on the way back), voiced slow over a sustained Polivoks-style drone pad with tape-delay feedback trails, the album's divided-city signature: the gesture of looking across the wall and being pulled back. Distinct in shape from any Sovetskiy motif (that album's tram-bell cell was a three-note 1 -> b3 -> 1 with no fourth-reach); this album's signature is the perfect-fourth reach across the line
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 1 plays it whole and unhurried, a single Polivoks lead voice over a drone pad with tape-delay trails, the dawn gaze across a city the listener already knows is split. Track 6 plays it bright and foreground at the actual line with the perfect-fourth reach LENGTHENED (1 -> 4 held a full bar) as the citizen gazes through the gap at the Western neon - the reach now literal, the fourth held open as long as the gaze lasts, then the minor-second fall pulls them back. Track 9 plays it fragmented to only the first two notes (1 -> 4) over and over with the return-leg removed entirely as the questioning lifts - the reach with no resolution, the released-but-not-freed gesture. Track 12 plays it whole one final time on Polivoks lead one octave down with the minor-second fall UNRESOLVED holding on the b3 rather than landing on the tonic - the second dawn's gaze across the same line, the divided mile re-read as a held breath that never exhales.

recurring_motifs.motif: Strip-light pulse - a two-note alternating dark-bass ostinato a tritone apart (1 <-> b5) on synth bass with a dry rim-click on each note, the unstable tritone alternation refusing to resolve to either pole, the album's death-strip / surveillance signature (the raked sand, the searchlight arm, the gray car three doors back - all the mechanisms that watch the line and never settle). Distinct from Sovetskiy's boiler-room pulse (that was a stable descending walking-bass 5-4-b3-1); this album's labor-and-watch figure is an unstable tritone oscillation, the sound of a thing that watches but never moves
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 3 introduces it clean as the watchtower and death-strip first enter the frame, the tritone bass alternation 1 <-> b5 with a single dry rim-click on each note, the strip beginning to watch. Track 5 makes it the structural backbone of the checkpoint-inspection chorus: the tritone alternation locked in tight with a stamp-and-teletype rhythm and the rim-click stiffened with a dry CR-78-cold drum-machine kick on the downbeat, the bureaucratic machine of the crossing made literal. Track 7 plays the tritone alternation accelerated to eighth notes with the rim-click doubled and a tape-delay smear on every b5 - the tail is on them, the watch has gone mobile and quickened. Track 10 plays only the first note (the tonic pole) of the alternation with the b5 removed entirely and no rim-click - the strip is behind them now, the tritone has finally collapsed to its single stable pole on the homeward train, the watching has stopped.

recurring_motifs.motif: Far-station bleed - a slow descending three-note Polivoks lead phrase (5 -> 4 -> b3) doubled an octave up with heavy tape hiss and a faint AM-radio band-noise wash riding under it, the album's forbidden-Western-signal signature (the contraband radio voice that bleeds through from the far sector, the bright commercial sound the East is not supposed to receive, the descent of the phrase mirroring the unreachable West always sliding back out of grasp). Distinct from Sovetskiy's Iron Curtain flag (that was a chromatic b2 -> 1 -> 7 -> b6 escalation gesture with church-bell strikes); this album's escalation/longing gesture is a diatonic descent drenched in radio bleed, the sound of the West heard but not held
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 2 introduces it once on the elevated S-Bahn as a faint descending Polivoks phrase under heavy tape hiss and AM band-noise, the Western station catching for a few bars through the carriage windows that face the far side. Track 6 brings it bright and foreground at the line: the descending phrase doubled an octave up and the AM band-noise wash lifted nearly to the level of the lead as the citizen stands at the gap and the actual Western commercial broadcast pours across the divide - the forbidden signal at its loudest and clearest, paired with the crossing-signal cell's longest reach. Track 8 plays the phrase slowed to half-speed and detuned a quarter-tone flat under the interview-room bulb with the tape hiss swelling - the far station now warped and accusatory, the bright Western sound turned into evidence of what the citizen was caught listening to. Track 11 plays the phrase one final time as the contraband radio is switched off: the descending Polivoks phrase decays into pure tape hiss and the AM band-noise collapses to a dead-carrier hum, the far station silenced by a hand on a dial, the West gone dark in the single room.

tracks.composer_notes: Sets the tonic (G minor) - a whole tone above Sovetskiy Vol. 1's F minor home, claiming its own harmonic home base distinct from the prior Eastern-Bloc volume so the two Cold-War-East albums never share a tonic. CROSSING-SIGNAL CELL introduced whole and clean on Polivoks-style lead over a drone pad with tape-delay trails - this is the album's thesis statement, the divided-city gesture of looking across the line. Its shape (1 -> 4 -> b3 -> 1, a perfect-fourth reach and a minor-second fall) is deliberately distinct from Sovetskiy's three-note tram-bell cell (1 -> b3 -> 1, no fourth-reach) so the two albums do not share a primary motif. NO KICK ANYWHERE on this track - the rhythmic engine is dry mechanical CR-78-cold drum machine with sparse rim-shot and closed hi-hat plus dark bass, never a synthwave four-on-the-floor and never a warm 808 boom. The faint distant-radio bleed under tape hiss is the album's atmospheric thesis - the forbidden Western signal that recurs as the far-station-bleed motif. Outro ends on G tonic (not the dominant) - the day begins on stillness. Track 2 takes the V (D) of G minor and reroots it as Dm for the S-Bahn scene.

tracks.composer_notes: COMEDOWN. FAR-STATION BLEED plays one FINAL time and then is SWITCHED OFF mid-phrase - the descending Polivoks phrase decays into pure tape hiss and the AM band-noise collapses to a dead-carrier hum, the far station silenced by a hand on a dial, the West gone dark in the single room. This is the resolution of the radio-bleed motif: the forbidden signal that opened the album as a faint dawn bleed is extinguished here at late night. After the switch-off, chorus2 and the outro carry only the dead-carrier hum where the far station was - do not let MusicGen re-introduce the bleed after the switch-off; its absence is the point. 240s long. Outro C (V of Fm) primes the perfect-fifth descent into Gm of track 12, closing the ring on the home tonic.

tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fifth from Fm to Gm... no - the fifth step would be Cm; instead the album closes the ring by stepping the flat-side descent's final move to the home tonic: C (the V that closed track 11) resolves directly to its own tonic-of-the-key reading as the dominant of nothing and the album simply lands on Gm, the home tonic that opened the record. Twelve tracks have traversed a sharp-side cycle-of-fifths-ascending ladder through the day (Gm to C#m, six perfect-fifth steps) and a flat-side cycle-of-fifths-descending ladder through the night (Abm to Ebm to Bbm to Fm, four perfect-fifth steps), and the closer lands the ring on the opening Gm. The listener arrives at the second dawn from the flat side of the loop, having descended the ladder the day climbed.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -2 BPM. Returns exactly to the opener's 80 BPM - the second gray dawn at the same tempo as the first, the ring closed on tempo as well as on tonic. Unlike Sovetskiy (which ended one tick below its opener), this album lands exactly on its opening tempo: the divided mile is walked again at the same pace, the loop is exact.
tracks.composer_notes: CLOSER. CROSSING-SIGNAL CELL RETURNS WHOLE ONE OCTAVE DOWN ON POLIVOKS LEAD with the minor-second fall UNRESOLVED holding on the b3 rather than landing on the tonic - the second dawn's gaze across the same line, the divided mile re-read as a held breath that never exhales. The crucial detail at the closer's open: the faint distant-radio bleed that opened track 1 does NOT return here - the radio was switched off in track 11, and its absence (only tape hiss and a dead-carrier hum remain) is the one thing that changed across the night. The forecourt is the same, the sodium lamp is the same, the wall is still standing - but the West is silent now. 300s long to give the coda its full held-breath time. The final 16-bar coda is the crossing-signal cell alone over fading drone pad and dark bass with the dead-carrier hum fading last - do not let MusicGen add a fade-up tail, a stinger, a re-introduced radio bleed, or any rhythmic figure in the final 16 bars; the unresolved Polivoks b3 over the fading pad to silence IS the composition. The album ends on Gm - the same tonic that opened it - but the fall is unresolved, signaling the next night's crossing is already coming. The ring is closed; the citizen is not. Do not let MusicGen substitute a kick drum or a PWM synthwave lead anywhere - the closer must remain in the album's coldwave voice palette of Polivoks sawtooth drone and lead, tape-delay trails, dark synth bass, dry mechanical CR-78-cold rim-shot, rim-click, and tape hiss.

album_notes.overall_form: Single night-and-day through the divided city over 12 tracks - gray dawn to second gray dawn - one citizen of the East side moving across and back from a concrete line they can see but never cross, circa 1983. This is a STANDALONE album, NOT part of the Lost Worlds saga and carrying NO saga leitmotifs. Three arcs: Gray waking (tracks 1-3): sodium-lamp dawn over a wet Plattenbau forecourt with a faint forbidden Western radio bleed, a near-empty S-Bahn over the elevated track past Westward-facing windows, the long avenue toward the wall-zone as the death-strip and watchtower enter the frame. Tempo climbs in small +2 BPM increments from 80 to 84 BPM. The wall and the checkpoint (tracks 4-6): the inner control-strip with its searchlight arm sweeping raked sand, the documents-and-mirror-pole inspection at the sector crossing over a stamp-and-teletype rhythm, the long gaze through a gap at the bright Western neon twenty meters away. Tempo climbs to the emotional/harmonic peak at 90 BPM (track 6, the gaze across the divide). Surveillance crisis and the long walk home (tracks 7-12): a tail picks the citizen up and a gray car keeps three doors back at the album's BPM ceiling (track 7, 92 BPM), a bare interview room under a single bulb with a reel-to-reel turning behind a one-way pane (track 8, 88 BPM, the flat-side enharmonic pivot), returned to the street with no explanation (track 9, 86 BPM exhale), the S-Bahn home through sleet (track 10, 84 BPM), the contraband radio switched off in a single room (track 11, 82 BPM), the second gray dawn over the same forecourt (track 12, 80 BPM, ring closed on opener tempo and tonic). The album splits its two peaks deliberately: the EMOTIONAL/HARMONIC peak is track 6 (the long gaze across the line, 90 BPM, Phrygian-dominant brightness) and the TEMPO/BPM ceiling is track 7 (the surveillance tail, 92 BPM, stark Aeolian), because looking across the wall and being followed are two different kinds of intensity and the slower-but-brighter gaze must not be the fastest moment. The album ends on Gm - the same tonic and the same tempo that opened it - but the crossing-signal cell's minor-second fall is left unresolved and the Western radio that opened the record has been switched off, signaling the next night's crossing is already coming. The ring is closed on tonic and tempo; the citizen is not closed on resolution.
album_notes.key_strategy: Sharp-side cycle-of-fifths ascending through six perfect-fifth steps for the day hours: Gm -> Dm -> Am (Phrygian b2) -> Em -> Bm (Dorian raised-6) -> F#m (Phrygian-dominant flavor). Modal shadings progress from plain minor (Gm, Dm, Em) through the wall-zone's clinical Phrygian (Am) and the checkpoint's Dorian raised-6 clinical glare (Bm) into the gaze-peak's aching Phrygian-dominant brightness (F#m). The Phrygian-dominant flavor in F#m at the gaze-peak (track 6) is the album's brightest harmonic gesture and reads as the Western neon seen across the line layered over East-side coldwave dryness, not as Phrygian metal. The crisis peak (track 7, C#m) strips the modal brightness away to stark Aeolian as the tail closes in. The descent flips to flat-side cycle-of-fifths descending through four perfect-fifth steps for the night hours: C#m -> Abm (enharmonic G#m pivot, the harmonic hinge of the whole record) -> Ebm -> Bbm -> Fm, and the closer lands the ring on the home tonic Gm. NO MAJOR TONICS appear on the album - this music lives in minor and modal-minor, never in plain major. The home tonic G minor is a WHOLE TONE above Sovetskiy Vol. 1's F minor home, deliberately claiming its own harmonic home base so the two Cold-War-East albums in the catalog never share a tonic. Where Sovetskiy ascended eight fifths and descended three (a sharp-heavy 24-hour kombinat loop), this album ascends six and descends four (a more balanced day-out-and-night-back arc with its hinge-pivot at the dead center), so the two albums read as distinct journeys through superficially adjacent aesthetics. The album never resolves to a comfortable harmonic frame; the closer's unresolved Polivoks b3 over Gm is the final harmonic gesture.
album_notes.tempo_strategy: Coldwave tempo range 80-92 BPM, sitting in the same dragging post-punk-coldwave territory as Sovetskiy (78-92) but with its floor raised two ticks to 80 so EVERY track is safely inside the nightdrive-llm::validate_spec 80-118 bound (Sovetskiy's track 12 at 78 needed a relaxed lower bound; this album needs none). Day accelerando: 80 -> 82 -> 84 -> 86 -> 88 -> 90 in strict +2 increments across tracks 1-6, the citizen moving from the pre-dawn forecourt through the train and the avenue to the wall and the checkpoint and the long gaze. Emotional/harmonic peak: 90 BPM (track 6, the gaze across the divide). BPM ceiling: 92 BPM (track 7, the surveillance tail) - a single +2 step above the gaze-peak, the album's only track at 92 and its highest tempo, reached at the paranoid crisis rather than the emotional peak. Night descent: 88 -> 86 -> 84 -> 82 -> 80 in strict -2 increments across tracks 8-12 (with the single -4 jump at track 7-to-8 from 92 to 88 marking the crisis breaking into the interview-room shoulder), mirroring the day accelerando step size in reverse. Ends EXACTLY on the opener's 80 BPM - the ring closed on tempo as well as tonic, the second dawn walked at the same pace as the first. The mechanical sidechain-pumping BPM range of synthwave (104+ BPM with hard-kick four-on-the-floor) is absent - this album lives below it, in coldwave / post-punk-coldwave tempo territory, the same shelf as Sovetskiy. The one dry kick on the whole album is the single downbeat thud in track 5's checkpoint inspection (never four-on-the-floor); every other track has NO kick.
album_notes.motif_strategy: Three motifs, each with four structural appearances, each deliberately distinct in SHAPE from Sovetskiy's three motifs so the two Cold-War-East albums share no melodic DNA. (1) Crossing-signal cell - a four-note Polivoks lead figure (1 -> 4 -> b3 -> 1, a perfect-fourth reach across the line and a minor-second fall pulling back), introduced WHOLE in track 1 (thesis, the dawn gaze across the split city), returned BRIGHT AND FOREGROUND with the perfect-fourth reach LENGTHENED to a full held bar in track 6 (the gaze through the gap at the Western neon, the reach made literal), played FRAGMENTED to only the first two notes (1 -> 4) with the return-leg removed in track 9 (the questioning lifts, the reach with no resolution), and played WHOLE ONE OCTAVE DOWN WITH THE FALL UNRESOLVED holding on the b3 in track 12 (closer, the second dawn's gaze, the held breath that never exhales). The four appearances mark the album's phase transitions: dawn (1), the line (6), release (9), second dawn (12). Its perfect-fourth-reach shape is deliberately distinct from Sovetskiy's three-note tram-bell cell (1 -> b3 -> 1, no fourth-reach). (2) Strip-light pulse - a two-note alternating dark-bass tritone ostinato (1 <-> b5) with a dry rim-click, the unstable tritone refusing to resolve, the death-strip/surveillance signature, introduced CLEAN in track 3 (the watchtower and strip enter the frame), moved to STRUCTURAL BACKBONE locked with the stamp-and-teletype rhythm and a stiffened kick in track 5 (the checkpoint inspection), played ACCELERATED to eighth notes with a tape-delay smear on every b5 in track 7 (the tail, the watch gone mobile), and COLLAPSED to only its single tonic pole with the tritone removed in track 10 (the homeward train, the watching stopped). Its unstable-tritone-oscillation shape is deliberately distinct from Sovetskiy's stable descending walking-bass boiler-room pulse (5-4-b3-1). (3) Far-station bleed - a descending three-note Polivoks phrase (5 -> 4 -> b3) doubled an octave up under tape hiss and AM band-noise, the forbidden-Western-signal signature, introduced once on the S-Bahn in track 2 (the station catches through the Westward windows), brought BRIGHT AND FOREGROUND at full volume in track 6 (the actual Western broadcast pouring across the gap, paired with the crossing-signal cell's longest reach), returned SLOWED to half-speed and DETUNED a quarter-tone flat in track 8 (the interview room, the bright sound warped into accusatory evidence), and SWITCHED OFF mid-phrase decaying into a dead-carrier hum in track 11 (the contraband radio silenced by a hand on a dial). Its diatonic-descent-drenched-in-radio-bleed shape is deliberately distinct from Sovetskiy's chromatic Iron Curtain flag escalation (b2 -> 1 -> 7 -> b6 with church bells). The far-station bleed's switch-off in track 11 is the album's quietest and most important transformation: the forbidden signal that opened the record is extinguished, and its ABSENCE at the track-12 closer (where track 1 had it present) is the one thing that changed across the divided mile.
album_notes.tracklist_continuity: Each track ends on a pitch that primes the next track's tonic via dominant relationship (V of next tonic), with one enharmonic pivot move at the crisis-to-night boundary (track 7 to 8). Specifically: 1 ends on Gm tonic (no V - the album begins on stillness; primes the perfect-fifth ascent to Dm). 2 ends on A (V of Dm, also tonic of track 3 reread as Am). 3 ends on E (V of Am, also tonic of track 4 reread as Em). 4 ends on B (V of Em, also tonic of track 5 reread as Bm). 5 ends on F# (V of Bm, also tonic of track 6 reread as F#m). 6 ends on C# (V of F#m, also tonic of track 7 reread as C#m). 7 ends on G# (V of C#m, reread enharmonically as Ab tonic of track 8 - the flat-side pivot that begins the night descent at the interview room, the harmonic hinge of the whole album). 8 ends on Eb (V of Abm, also tonic of track 9 reread as Ebm). 9 ends on Bb (V of Ebm, also tonic of track 10 reread as Bbm). 10 ends on F (V of Bbm, also tonic of track 11 reread as Fm). 11 ends on C (V of Fm, primes the perfect-fifth descent into Gm of track 12 closing the ring on the home tonic). The listener will not consciously hear most of these pivots - they will only feel that each transition is inevitable, and that the night descent's harmonic logic reverses the day's ascent by direction of cycle-of-fifths motion. The single enharmonic pivot (G# = Ab) at track 7-to-8 is the structural hinge where the day's sharp-side ascent flips to the night's flat-side descent, placed at the dead center of the record at the interview-room crisis-break.
album_notes.visual_continuity_notes: Twelve covers follow the album's narrative arc as a single night-and-day through the divided city, gray dawn to second gray dawn, circa 1983. The aesthetic vocabulary is locked and distinct from Sovetskiy's Soviet-kombinat palette: cold pewter-gray concrete and wet asphalt and prefab-panel facades, sodium-amber streetlamp accents, hard-white floodlight and searchlight accents at the wall-zone, and CRUCIALLY the warm-amber-and-soft-pink Western neon glow seen ONLY across the line (only through the gap in track 6 and only as a blurred smear through sleet-streaked glass in track 10 - never on the near East side, which stays cold gray). No full cyberpunk cyan flood (Tron / Neo-Tokyo territory), no warm sunset magenta (Sunset territory), no Tang-orange suburb (Atompunk-West territory), no red-star emblems (those were Sovetskiy's Soviet markers - this divided-city album avoids them to stay distinct), no people visible, no faces, no legible text or signage of any kind (no German lettering, no graffiti text - SDXL would render it garbled and the album avoids legible language entirely). Track 1: wet Plattenbau forecourt at gray dawn with a single sodium lamp (exterior). Track 2: near-empty elevated S-Bahn carriage with Westward-facing rain-flecked windows (interior). Track 3: long gray avenue toward the wall-zone with watchtower silhouette and raked-sand strip on the horizon (exterior wide). Track 4: the inner death-strip control-zone with raked sand and a sweeping searchlight beam (exterior, hero cover 1). Track 5: sector-crossing inspection lane with barrier arm and mirror-pole and control booth (exterior close). Track 6: the view through a gap in the wall toward distant warm Western neon glow (exterior, hero cover 2, the one cover where warm neon appears). Track 7: a gray two-stroke car following three doors back down a dusk avenue (exterior, point-of-view). Track 8: a bare interrogation room with a single hanging bulb and a one-way mirror with a reel-to-reel behind it (interior). Track 9: a narrow gray side street outside a steel door just ajar (exterior). Track 10: the same elevated carriage as track 2 now at night with sleet-streaked windows and blurred distant Western lights (interior). Track 11: a single spare room with a contraband radio whose dial-glow is dying (interior). Track 12: the same Plattenbau forecourt as track 1 at a second gray dawn with the same sodium lamp and the puddles skinned with thin ice (exterior). The architectural rhyme of tracks 1 and 12 (same forecourt, same lamp, second dawn - the puddles now iced), tracks 2 and 10 (same elevated carriage, dawn vs night-through-sleet), and the wall-zone cluster of tracks 3-4-5-6 (avenue, strip, checkpoint, gap) is the album's visual structure. All cover prompts front-load aggressive SDXL text-suppression in the first 50 words: 'no text, no words, no lettering, no typography, no font, no characters, no labels, no signage with readable words, no propaganda text, no graffiti text, no people visible, no faces' - placed BEFORE the scene description because CLIP truncates at 77 tokens and tail-positioned negatives are dropped. All cover prompts are written for SDXL native generation at 1024x1024, 1344x768, and 768x1344 - scene depth (foreground + midground + background) survives reframing across all three aspect ratios, no pose-locked compositions.

album_notes.differentiation_from_prior_albums: The Divided Mile is a STANDALONE Cold-War-East-Berlin album (NOT part of the Lost Worlds saga, NO saga leitmotifs) that shares only the broad Eastern-Bloc / Cold-War era and coldwave tempo shelf with Sovetskiy Vol. 1 and is otherwise deliberately distinct from it and from every prior volume. Sunset Vol. 1 was warm Miami coastal-drive in synthwave palette (A minor, 82-112 BPM, sidechain four-on-the-floor). Tron Vol. 1 was cold Tron grid in cyan-magenta cyberpunk (A minor, 96-112 BPM, PWM saws). Neo-Tokyo Vol. 1 was Akira-coded vertical descent in red-orange-violet anime palette (D minor, 104-120 BPM, hard kick). Atompunk Vol. 1 was mid-century WESTERN Cold War 24-hour cycle in steel-grey/Tang-orange retrofuture (C minor, 84-98 BPM, Theremin + muted brass + vibraphone). Sovetskiy Vol. 1 was the EAST-SIDE Soviet 24-hour kombinat-courtyard loop in red-star/ice-blue palette (F minor, 78-92 BPM, Polivoks lead + Juno pad + TR-606-cold drum + walking bass + church bell). The Divided Mile is set NOT in a Soviet kombinat loop but in the DIVIDED CITY OF BERLIN circa 1983: a day-out-and-night-back crossing of a city split by a concrete line, with the death-strip, the watchtower, the checkpoint with its mirror-pole, the gaze across the gap at Western neon, the surveillance tail, the interview room, and the contraband Western radio as its specific imagery. Home tonic G minor is a WHOLE TONE above Sovetskiy's F minor, so the two Cold-War-East albums never share a tonic. The voice palette is COLDWAVE-BERLIN distinct from Sovetskiy's Soviet-ensemble palette: POLIVOKS-STYLE SAWTOOTH DRONE PAD AND LEAD with heavy TAPE-DELAY FEEDBACK TRAILS (where Sovetskiy used Juno-60 pad with long PLATE reverb - tape-delay vs plate reverb is the deliberate textural fork), DARK SYNTH BASS, DRY MECHANICAL CR-78-COLD DRUM MACHINE with sparse RIM-SHOT and closed hi-hat and NO KICK (where Sovetskiy used TR-606-cold with snare on 2-and-4 - CR-78 rim-shot vs TR-606 snare is the deliberate drum-machine fork), RIM-CLICK accents (where Sovetskiy used WOOD-BLOCK knocks), and HEAVY TAPE HISS plus AM-RADIO BAND-NOISE WASH as the album's atmospheric signature (where Sovetskiy used a deep dry CHURCH-BELL tone). NO kick drum anywhere except the single dry downbeat thud in track 5's checkpoint inspection (never four-on-the-floor). NO sidechain compression. NO PWM saws (Tron territory), no FM-bell synthwave accents (Sunset territory), no Theremin / muted brass / vibraphone / timpani (Atompunk-West territory), no church-bell tone or wood-block knock or Juno-plate-reverb (Sovetskiy territory), no red-star visual markers (Sovetskiy territory). The three motifs (crossing-signal cell, strip-light pulse, far-station bleed) are ALL distinct in melodic shape from Sovetskiy's three motifs (tram-bell cell, boiler-room pulse, Iron Curtain flag): a perfect-fourth-reach cell vs a no-reach three-note cell, an unstable-tritone oscillation vs a stable descending walking-bass, and a diatonic radio-bleed descent vs a chromatic church-bell escalation - so the two Cold-War-East albums share NO melodic DNA. Structurally the albums diverge too: Sovetskiy ascended eight fifths and descended three in a sharp-heavy 24-hour kombinat loop, while The Divided Mile ascends six and descends four in a more balanced day-out-night-back arc with its enharmonic hinge-pivot at the dead center (track 7-to-8). The album sits on the same shelf as the prior five volumes - same composer, same channel, same audience (late-night programmers, coders debugging, 2am study sessions: introspective, focused, never frantic) - but reads as its own distinct Cold-War-Berlin coldwave night, in cold pewter-gray and sodium-amber and forbidden-Western-neon-glimpsed-across-the-line palette. DANGER ZONE respected (danger_zone_keys berlin_wall + sovetskiy): NO proper nouns or track titles lifted from specific Cold-War films or their soundtrack albums - no 'Lives of Others' / 'Sonata for a Good Man' / 'Goodbye Lenin' / 'Deutschland 83' / 'Sterben fuer Berlin' / 'Wind of Change' / named Stasi-film cues; no Sovetskiy overlap (no 'Stalker' / 'Solaris' / 'Sputnik' / 'Vostok' / 'Chernobyl' / 'Pripyat' / 'Lenin' / 'Kremlin'); no real named checkpoints used as track titles (the literal name 'Checkpoint Charlie' from the theme brief is referenced only in the THEME field, never as a track title - track titles are generic-evocative: Sodium Dawn Forecourt, Elevated Line Westward Windows, Toward the Strip, Control-Strip Searchlight Arm, Documents Mirror-Pole, The Gap Western Neon, Three Doors Back, Bulb and Reel, Returned to the Street, Sleet on the Elevated Line, Dial-Glow Dies, Second Gray Dawn). Double-hit rule satisfied: no title is both a named object/dialogue in a specific Cold-War film AND a track on that film's canonical soundtrack album.
